subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Exact contents from the provided rulebook text: 1 Board; 7 Role cards; 7 Pawns; 59 Player cards (48 City cards, 6 Epidemic cards, 5 Event cards); 48 Infection cards; 96 Disease cubes (24 cubes in each of 4 colors); 6 Research stations; 4 Cure markers; 1 Outbreaks marker; 1 Infection rate marker; 4 Reference cards. The cure markers have a vial side and an eradication side.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: 1) Place the board where all players can reach it. 2) Put the 6 research stations and all disease cubes near the board, separating cubes into 4 color supply piles. 3) Place 1 research station in Atlanta. 4) Place the outbreaks marker on 0 of the Outbreaks Track. 5) Place the 4 cure markers vial-side-up near the Discovered Cure indicators. 6) Place the infection rate marker on the left-most '2' space of the Infection Rate Track. 7) Shuffle the Infection deck and infect 9 cities in setup order: draw 3 cards and place 3 matching cubes on each, then draw 3 cards and place 2 cubes each, then draw 3 cards and place 1 cube each (18 cubes total). Put those 9 infection cards face up in the Infection Discard Pile; the rest form the Infection Deck.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Give each player 1 Reference card. Shuffle Role cards and deal 1 face up to each player. Place each dealt role’s matching pawn in Atlanta. Remove unused Role cards and pawns from the game. Prepare Player cards by removing all 6 Epidemic cards first. Shuffle the remaining City + Event cards and deal starting hands based on player count: 2-player game = 4 cards each, 3-player game = 3 cards each, 4-player game = 2 cards each.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: After initial hands are dealt, set difficulty by choosing Epidemic count: 4 (Introductory), 5 (Standard), or 6 (Heroic). Remove unused Epidemic cards from the game. Divide the remaining Player cards into as many face-down piles as Epidemic cards in use, as equal as possible. Shuffle 1 Epidemic card into each pile, then stack the piles (smaller piles at bottom) to form the Player Deck. Players may review their initial City cards. The player holding the highest-population city card is first player. The first turn begins with the standard 3-part turn sequence.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
Each turn is divided into 3 numbered phases:
1. Do 4 actions.
2. Draw 2 Player cards. Resolve any Epidemic cards immediately as drawn. If hand size exceeds 7 at end of draws/resolution, discard down to 7 (or play Event cards as allowed) before continuing.
3. Infect cities according to current infection rate by flipping Infection cards and placing cubes.

After the active player finishes infecting cities, turn passes to the player on their left. The game is cooperative: all players may discuss and advise, but the active player makes final decisions on their turn.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
You may perform any combination of actions below, up to 4 total per turn. Repeating the same action is allowed; each use costs 1 action.

Movement actions:
- Drive / Ferry: Move to a city connected by a white line.
- Direct Flight: Discard a City card to move to the city named on that card.
- Charter Flight: Discard the City card matching your current city to move to any city.
- Shuttle Flight: Move from a city with a research station to another city with a research station.

Board/system note from rules: White-line routes can wrap around board edges (example in text: Sydney and Los Angeles are connected).

Other actions:
- Build a Research Station: Discard the City card matching your current city to place a research station there. If all 6 stations are already on board, move one existing station from anywhere to this city.
- Treat Disease: Remove 1 cube from your current city. If that disease has been cured, remove all cubes of that color in your current city with one Treat action.
- Share Knowledge: If both players are in the same city, either give the City card matching that city to the other player, or take that matching card from them. Both players must agree.
- Discover a Cure: At a research station, discard 5 City cards of the same color to cure that disease and move its cure marker to the cure indicator. Role note from provided text: Scientist needs only 4 cards of same color to Discover a Cure.

Card and discard handling:
- Cards discarded for actions go to the Player Discard Pile.
- If a player receives a card and exceeds 7 cards, they must immediately discard or play an Event card as allowed.

Eradication interaction:
- If the last cube of a cured disease is removed from board, flip that disease marker to eradicated side.
- Eradication is not required to win, but eradicated diseases no longer place cubes during infection/epidemic steps.

Important restriction:
- Removing the last cube of a disease that has NOT been cured does not eradicate it and gives no special benefit.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Epidemic sequence (as shown on Epidemic card text):
1) INCREASE: Move infection rate marker forward 1 space.
2) INFECT: Draw bottom card from Infection Deck, place 3 cubes on that city, discard that card to Infection Discard.
3) INTENSIFY: Shuffle Infection Discard Pile and place it on top of Infection Deck.

This creates core pressure: recently infected cities are likely to be hit again soon after epidemics.

Outbreak pressure and team-loss track:
- The game tracks outbreaks with a dedicated marker. If outbreaks reach 8, players lose immediately.

Role asymmetry:
- Each player role has unique ability modifications (example explicitly provided: Scientist discovers cures with 4 matching cards instead of 5). These role powers alter baseline efficiency and should be planned around in team strategy.

Event cards:
- Event cards are in the Player deck and can be played at any time per card timing text (examples shown in provided excerpt include effects like skipping infect step or removing infection discard cards from game). They are tempo tools and do not consume standard action slots unless card text states otherwise.

Co-op table protocol from rules:
- Open discussion is allowed and encouraged; however, the active player retains decision authority on their own turn.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
Pandemic uses binary team outcome (win/lose), not victory points.

Team win condition:
- Discover cures for all 4 diseases (Blue, Yellow, Black, Red).

Immediate team loss conditions from the provided rules:
1) 8 outbreaks occur (outbreak marker reaches the loss threshold).
2) Not enough disease cubes remain in supply when cubes must be placed.
3) Not enough Player cards remain when cards must be drawn (time runs out).

Eradication note:
- Eradicating diseases is optional and not required for victory, but it prevents new cube placement for that color and greatly reduces risk.

Scoring and tiebreakers:
- There are no VP sources, no final score table, and no tie-breakers because the game ends immediately on any win or loss trigger.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Stabilize the 3-cube setup cities first. Those are outbreak flashpoints, and one bad chain early can erase your margin.
2) Build a practical station network, not a decorative one. You already start in Atlanta with 1 station; your next station(s) should shorten travel to high-risk clusters and cure handoff points.
3) Decide cure priorities from opening hands immediately. Route players so matching colors can consolidate for Discover a Cure as soon as practical.
4) Use actions where they create tempo swings: treating a cured color is action-efficient (remove all cubes of that color in city), while untreated hot spots can trigger repeated reinfections after epidemics.
5) Preserve high-value movement cards when possible. Burning key city cards for flights can delay cures if those colors are already scarce in your team’s hands.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Over-treating low-risk cities while ignoring 3-cube clusters. This loses to outbreaks, not to neat board aesthetics.
2) Spending too many City cards on movement in the first few turns. If you discard cure-color cards for travel, you often stall discovery timing and lose to deck pressure.
3) Building research stations without a network plan. Stations are strongest when they create fast shuttle corridors for cures and crisis response, not just because you can build them.
4) Hoarding Event cards until 'perfect value' appears. Many losses come from dying with powerful Events unplayed; tempo now is often worth more than ideal timing later.
5) Ignoring the hand limit during card transfers and draw steps. Forced discards can delete cure progress if not planned in advance.
6) Treating eradication as mandatory before curing the next disease. Eradication is useful, but the win condition is all four cures; overcommitting to cleanup can cost the game clock.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
1) Containment vs cure-rush:
Do you spend actions now reducing cubes and outbreak risk, or push hard to finish a cure set? Correct choice depends on infection discard composition, board heat, and proximity to epidemic cycling.

2) Mobility vs card economy:
Direct and charter flights are powerful, but they consume city cards that might be cure-critical. Teams must decide when action tempo is worth card sacrifice.

3) Station placement and role allocation:
Where you build stations defines your long-term movement graph. Combined with role powers (like Scientist’s 4-card cures), this determines whether your team can both respond to outbreaks and finish cures in time.

4) Tactical event timing:
Event cards can flatten spikes from epidemics or preserve board stability between infect/intensify loops. The strategic trade-off is immediate stabilization versus saving options for future unknowns.

metadata.notes: Primary source used: Pandemic rulebook text provided in prompt (components, setup, action list, epidemic sequence, win/loss conditions).
